exercises.title: Tuple Unpacking Practice
exercises.description: Unpack a series of tuples and use the values. Unpack a coordinate pair, unpack a date, and use extended unpacking to separate the first and last from the middle.
exercises.starterCode:
# Coordinate pair
point = (3, 7)
# TODO: Unpack point into x and y, then print them

# Date tuple
date = (2024, 6, 15)
# TODO: Unpack date into year, month, day and print: "Year: 2024, Month: 6, Day: 15"

# Extended unpacking
scores = [95, 82, 78, 91, 67]
# TODO: Use extended unpacking to get first score, last score, and middle as a list
# Then print all three
exercises.expectedOutput:
x=3, y=7
Year: 2024, Month: 6, Day: 15
First: 95
Last: 67
Middle: [82, 78, 91]
hints: x, y = point unpacks two values
year, month, day = date unpacks three values
first, *middle, last = scores uses extended unpacking

exercises.title: Tuples as Dictionary Keys
exercises.description: Tuples are hashable and can be used as dictionary keys. Create a dictionary mapping (row, col) grid coordinates to values, then print the value at (1, 2) and check if (3, 3) is in the grid.
exercises.starterCode:
# TODO: Create a dict 'grid' mapping these (row, col) tuples to these values:
# (0, 0) -> 'start'
# (1, 2) -> 'treasure'
# (2, 1) -> 'trap'
# (3, 3) -> 'exit'


# TODO: Print the value at position (1, 2)

# TODO: Print whether (3, 3) is in the grid (True/False)

# TODO: Print whether (0, 5) is in the grid (True/False)
hints: grid[(1, 2)] accesses the value at that coordinate
Use 'in' to check if a key exists: (3, 3) in grid

exercises.title: Named Tuples for Records
exercises.description: Use namedtuple to create a Point type with x and y fields. Create three points, calculate the distance from the origin for each, and print the point with the greatest distance.
exercises.starterCode:
from collections import namedtuple
import math

# TODO: Define a namedtuple 'Point' with fields 'x' and 'y'

# TODO: Create these three points:
# p1 = Point(3, 4)
# p2 = Point(1, 1)
# p3 = Point(5, 12)


# TODO: For each point, calculate distance from origin: sqrt(x^2 + y^2)
# Print: "Point(x=3, y=4) distance: 5.0"

# TODO: Print the point with the greatest distance from origin
exercises.expectedOutput:
Point(x=3, y=4) distance: 5.0
Point(x=1, y=1) distance: 1.4142135623730951
Point(x=5, y=12) distance: 13.0
Farthest: Point(x=5, y=12)
hints: Use max() with a key function to find the farthest point
Access fields by name: p1.x and p1.y

warmup.title: Quick Recall: Tuple Immutability
warmup.description: What does this code print? Notice what is and isn't allowed with tuples.
warmup.starterCode:
t = (10, 20, 30)
print(t[0])
print(t[-1])
print(len(t))
a, b, c = t
print(a, b, c)
